name: Store Norske Leif Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced serif typeface derived from the Leif family, which itself draws on Times interpretations by Simoncini, Novarese and Fenocchio. The monospaced format and serif construction place it clearly in the serif mono subcategory.
summary: A monospaced serif companion to Store Norske Leif, carrying the warmth and playful character of its parent family into technical and editorial contexts.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Leif Mono inherits the expressive personality of Store Norske Leif, itself informed by three notable interpretations of Times by Francesco Simoncini, Aldo Novarese and Umberto Fenocchio. Where Times is typically read as cold and functional, Leif Mono brings a sense of warmth and character to the fixed-pitch format. It covers an extensive Latin character set with support for Nordic and Sami languages, and includes a range of OpenType features including oldstyle figures, fractions, stylistic alternates and localised forms.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Leif Mono as a monospaced companion to Store Norske Leif, a typeface born from an exploration of three interpretations of Times by Simoncini, Novarese and Fenocchio. They characterise it as retaining the essential structure of Times while replacing its coldness with warmth, joy and expressive charm, making communication feel inviting and joyful.

name: Store Norske Skandia
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a rational geometric typeface rooted in Scandinavian modernism, with neo-grotesque origins traced to a Norwegian National Railway typeface; the combination of geometric construction and neo-grotesque heritage places it squarely in geometric sans territory.
summary: A geometric sans rooted in Scandinavian modernism, reviving a neo-grotesque originally created for the Norwegian National Railway.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Skandia draws on a rediscovered neo-grotesque from the Norwegian National Railway to build a rational, clean-lined geometric sans suited to branding, advertising and digital work. The superfamily spans four subfamilies — standard, Narrow, Condensed and Mono — each offered in eight weights with matching italics, giving the system considerable range across widths and applications. Latin coverage is broad, extending from Western and Central European to Nordic, Baltic, Celtic and Sami languages.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Skandia as a rational geometric typeface built for the northern mindset, rooted in the clarity and utility of Scandinavian modernism. Its straightforward forms and understated elegance are designed to convey simplicity and functionality, making it a reliable tool for branding, advertising and digital design. The family began with the discovery of a long-lost Scandinavian neo-grotesque originally designed for the Norwegian National Railway.

name: Store Norske Leif
classification_notes: Leif draws directly from three Italian interpretations of Times New Roman, retaining the transitional serif structure of its source material while introducing asymmetrical contrast and rounder forms that soften the rational coldness of Times.
summary: A Times-derived serif by Arve Båtevik that softens the rationalism of its source material with asymmetrical contrast, rounder capitals and a lively italic rhythm.
description: Drawing on three Italian interpretations of Times by Francesco Simoncini, Aldo Novarese and Umberto Fenocchio, Leif preserves the structural versatility of Times while introducing rounder shapes, subtle irregularities and an asymmetrical contrast that gives text a more fluid, approachable cadence. Named after Norwegian comedian Leif Juster, the typeface is aimed at branding, digital projects and body text where a familiar serif structure needs a warmer, more expressive character. A monospaced companion, Leif Mono, extends the family with a typewriter-inspired form carrying the same sensibility.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Leif as a serif channelling warmth, joy and personality, born from an exploration of three notable Times interpretations. They position it as a refreshing alternative to more restrained serifs, designed to spread joy in branding and digital contexts, with its asymmetrical contrast and pre-Times-inspired capitals making every composition feel both familiar and delightfully new.

name: Store Norske Skandia Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width companion to a neo-grotesque sans, retaining balanced contrast and geometric construction from the parent family within a monospaced structure.
summary: A monospaced companion to the Skandia neo-grotesque family, designed by Arve Båtevik and rooted in a long-lost Norwegian railway typeface.
description: Derived from Skandia, which originates from a Scandinavian neo-grotesque designed for the Norwegian National Railway, this monospaced variant preserves the parent family's balanced contrast and geometric construction while placing those forms into a fixed-width structure. The regular interval of monospaced setting brings alignment and repetition into sharper focus, producing a denser text image with a more deliberate rhythm between letter and space. Language support extends across Western, Central and Northern European scripts including Nordic and Sami languages, with a full complement of OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Skandia Mono as carrying the same clear and measured voice as Skandia into a more regular structure, where the fixed-width format sharpens the compactness already present in the original. They note that the balanced contrast and geometric construction remain close to the parent family, while the monospaced setting brings interval, alignment and repetition more clearly into view, extending the family's range without breaking its character.

name: Store Norske Ja
classification_notes: Low-contrast geometric sans-serif with clean, friendly forms and generous x-height; deliberately avoids eccentric details in favour of broad usability across screen and print contexts.
summary: A low-contrast geometric sans by Arve Båtevik, built for digital brand use across dense and minimal layouts.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Ja is a geometric sans-serif that keeps its forms clean and uneccentric, with a generous x-height and balanced proportions suited to screen environments. The name draws on the Norwegian word for 'yes', framing the typeface's adaptability to different tones and contexts. A monospaced companion, Ja Mono, shares the same character, and a custom version was developed for Scribd.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Ja as a low-contrast geometric sans-serif built for versatility, clarity and approachability. Inspired by the adaptable nature of the Norwegian word 'Ja' (yes), it is intended to serve both dense informational layouts and expressive, minimalist applications, with a broad Latin character set covering Nordic, Baltic, Celtic and Central European languages among others.

name: Store Norske Gangster
classification_notes: Gangster draws directly from Renaissance sources including Garamond, Granjon and Grandjean, placing it firmly in the humanist serif tradition. Its distillation of complex Renaissance curves into cleaner digital forms places it close to contemporary neo-humanist interpretations of that lineage.
summary: A Renaissance-informed serif by Arve Båtevik, drawing on Garamond, Granjon and Grandjean to produce clean, purposeful letterforms suited to book publishing and branding.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik for Store Norske Skriftkompani, Gangster refines the intricate curves of Renaissance letterforms into digital shapes that retain historical character without nostalgia. The family includes a rich set of OpenType features covering small caps, multiple figure styles, swashes and ten stylistic sets in italic. Language support spans a broad Latin character set reaching Western and Central European, Nordic, Baltic, Celtic and Sami scripts.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Gangster as a Renaissance-inspired typeface drawing on the influence of Garamond, Jannon, Granjon and Grandjean. They present it as balancing historical complexity with modern simplicity, channelling the spirit of Renaissance letterforms through a digital lens, and position it for book publishing, editorial work, branding and digital projects.

name: Store Norske Minimal
classification_notes: Near-monolinear strokes and strong modular construction place this firmly in the geometric sans-serif tradition, inspired by utilitarian signage and industrial lettering rather than humanist or grotesque forms.
summary: A constructed geometric sans by Arve Båtevik, drawn from the utilitarian logic of number plates and street signs for use in identities, interfaces and signage systems.
description: Taking its formal cues from industrial lettering, Minimal uses near-monolinear strokes and modular construction to produce a typeface that reads as manufactured without feeling mechanical. Optical corrections are deliberately restrained, keeping the underlying geometric logic visible in each form. It is pitched at technology, engineering, science and public information contexts where structure and legibility need to carry across different scales and formats.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Minimal as a constructed geometric sans serif developed for branding, information, and visual systems, with a point of departure in the utilitarian lettering of number plates, street signs, and industrial panels. They note its near-monolinear strokes, modular elements, and strong geometric logic, while pointing to small adjustments that keep it from becoming inert. It is positioned for identities, signage, interfaces, and communication systems shaped by technology, engineering, science, sportswear, AI, robotics, and public information.

name: Store Norske Nora
classification_notes: Constructed from repeating geometric modules that evoke porcelain forms, with variable-font spacing from wide to condensed; the design is explicitly display-oriented with a compact Latin character set and no text-setting intent.
summary: A variable geometric display typeface by Arve Båtevik, reviving and expanding the modular lettering alphabet that Norwegian industrial designer Nora Gulbrandsen devised for Porsgrunds Porselænsfabrik in the 1930s.
description: Constructed from repeating modular components shaped to suggest plates and porcelain objects, the letterforms shift horizontally across a variable axis from widely spaced to near-conventional condensed configurations. The design takes Gulbrandsen's concept of 'formlek', playful experimentation with form, as its guiding principle rather than strict functional geometry. The character set covers Western European and Nordic languages including Norwegian, Danish and Swedish, with OpenType stylistic sets for alternate I, J, R and Ø forms.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Nora as a reinterpretation and expansion of industrial designer Nora Gulbrandsen's lettering alphabet from the 1930s. Gulbrandsen, chief designer at Porsgrunds Porselænsfabrik from 1929 to 1945, characterised her method as 'formlek', meaning playful experimentation with forms. The typeface is built geometrically from modular parts evocative of porcelain objects, and as a variable font it allows the letterforms to shift horizontally from wide to condensed. The foundry note its popularity in branding and editorial contexts for its strong graphic character.

name: Store Norske Arkitekt
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly described as geometric, drawing on pure circular and rectilinear forms from Norwegian functionalist architecture and historical geometric lettering alphabets of the 1920s–30s.
summary: A geometric sans by Arve Båtevik rooted in Norwegian functionalist architecture and the lettering traditions of 1920s–30s Nordic building signage.
description: Drawing on historical alphabets by Trygve M. Davidsen and Per Grieg, alongside the forms of landmark Norwegian functionalist buildings, the typeface applies the period's governing concerns of clarity, proportion and practical reasoning to a contemporary type system. Rather than simple pastiche, it is grounded in pre-functionalist typographic knowledge, giving it the structural confidence the best architects of that era brought to their work. Suited to identities, signage, exhibitions and institutional communication with ties to architecture and public culture.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Arkitekt as a geometric typeface inspired by Norwegian functionalist architecture, shaped by the lettering found on its buildings and rooted in the typographic knowledge that predates functionalism. They cite Trygve M. Davidsen's geometric alphabets, Per Grieg's building lettering and landmark buildings by Lars Backer and others as reference points, and position the typeface for identities, signage, exhibitions and institutional communication connected to architecture and public space.

name: Store Norske Nord Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced companion to the Nord grotesque family, retaining the turn-of-the-century grotesque character of the original with trumpet-shaped stroke endings adapted to fixed-width settings.
summary: A monospaced companion to the Nord grotesque family, designed by Arve Båtevik with trumpet-shaped stroke endings and eight weights for coding, editorial and branding use.
description: Nord Mono carries the northern, turn-of-the-century grotesque character of the Nord family into a fixed-width format, with matching italics across eight weights from Thin to Black. Trumpet-shaped stroke endings give it a quiet distinctiveness that sets it apart from more anonymous coding faces. The extensive Latin character set covers Nordic and Sami languages, and five stylistic sets offer alternates for characters including the lowercase 'a', zero and percent sign.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Nord Mono as a monospaced companion to the Nord family, retaining the same northern interpretation of turn-of-the-century grotesque in a typewriter format. They highlight its eight weights with matching italics, broad Latin language support covering Nordic and Sami scripts, and its suitability across coding environments, editorial layouts and branding systems requiring both precision and personality.

name: Store Norske Nord
classification_notes: A turn-of-the-century grotesque with trumpet-shaped stroke endings, rooted in Oslo's signage tradition from 1864 to 1964; sits squarely in the grotesque subgenre with regional Nordic character.
summary: A grotesque with trumpet-shaped stroke endings, designed by Arve Båtevik from Oslo's signage tradition spanning 1864 to 1964.
description: Conceived for the Skrift i Oslo exhibition, Nord draws on a century of Oslo signage lettering to produce a distinctly regional take on the turn-of-the-century grotesque. Eight weights with matching italics cover text and editorial needs, while Nord Plakat extends the family into a display cut informed by 1960s modernist poster lettering, where custom-drawn grotesques compensated for the absence of large-format type. A monospaced companion, Nord Mono, completes the system with the same character in a typewriter-inspired format.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Nord as a northern interpretation of the classic grotesque, defined by trumpet-shaped stroke endings and conceived for an exhibition exploring Oslo's signage tradition from 1864 to 1964. The family spans eight weights with italics for text and editorial use, a poster-focused display cut called Nord Plakat inspired by 1960s modernist letterers, and Nord Mono for monospaced applications. Together they position the family as equally suited to running text, branding systems, posters and digital environments.

name: Store Norske Nord Plakat
classification_notes: Explicitly positioned as a display cut of a grotesque family, drawn from 1960s poster lettering with heavier weight and greater graphic density than a text grotesque; fits display rather than sans-serif as its primary purpose is large-scale poster and title use.
summary: A heavy display cut drawn from 1960s Scandinavian poster lettering, giving any word the presence of custom-drawn type at large scale.
description: Developed from the bold hand-drawn lettering of 1960s posters, where desired typefaces were unavailable in sizes suitable for direct setting and words had to be drawn, enlarged and spaced by eye. Nord Plakat retains the northern grotesque character of the Nord family but pushes the forms toward greater weight, density and graphic force, so that individual letters carry a composition rather than merely occupying it. Suited to posters, exhibition graphics, campaign titles and large-scale identity work where type needs to function as a primary visual element.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Nord Plakat as the most explicitly graphic member of the Nord family, developed from the bold lettering of 1960s posters where words had to be drawn, enlarged and adapted for the poster surface by eye and direct graphic judgement. The typeface gathers a wide range of poster lettering into a unified cut with greater weight, density and graphic force than the base family, designed so that any word takes on the presence of custom lettering drawn for that specific use.

name: Store Norske Magi
classification_notes: Explicitly described as geometrically constructed with a monolinear structure and narrow proportions, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with a Scandinavian civic character.
summary: A geometrically constructed sans from Norwegian designer Arve Båtevik, built for civic communication, branding and screen environments with narrow proportions and a monolinear rhythm.
description: Drawn in the Scandinavian modern tradition, Magi takes its cues from public information typography: orderly, measured and quietly assured. Its narrow proportions and even monolinear structure give it a compact rhythm suited to interfaces, signage and identity systems where legibility and tone need to stay aligned across contexts. A monospaced companion, Magi Mono, extends the family into typewriter-inspired territory, while broad language support covers Western and Northern European scripts including Nordic and Sami languages.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Magi as a geometrically constructed sans serif designed for branding, public information and screen reading, rooted in the visual language of civic institutions and public culture. Its narrow proportions, monolinear structure and full range of weights and italics allow it to move from navigation and display to continuous reading, with Magi Mono offering a typewriter-inspired companion for those needing a monospaced variant.

name: Store Norske Eleganse
classification_notes: Rooted in Ehmcke's Elzevir (1927), Eleganse blends ornamental calligraphic tradition with geometric structure, placing it firmly in the display category with a calligraphic subcategory.
summary: A display typeface by Arve Båtevik that revives Ehmcke's 1927 Elzevir, balancing ornamental calligraphic letterforms with a rational, contemporary structure.
description: Drawing on the legacy of Ehmcke's Elzevir, first published by Ludwig Wagner in 1927, this face reinterprets early 20th-century calligraphic tradition through measured, modern construction. The design pairs monumental, decorative capitals with fluid lowercase letterforms, and ten stylistic sets allow designers to moderate or amplify its eccentricity. Broad Latin coverage spans Western and Central European scripts through to Nordic and Sami languages.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Eleganse as a display typeface embodying expressive artistry and refined craftsmanship, rooted in Ehmcke's Elzevir. They frame it as a reinterpretation of early 20th-century calligraphic tradition through a contemporary lens, suited to branding and editorial projects that call for visual impact and individuality.

name: Store Norske Magi Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface with monolinear structure and soft geometry, designed as a monospaced companion to Magi, intended for code, editorial and interface use.
summary: A fixed-width companion to Magi by Arve Båtevik, carrying the same open and measured character into a regular, compact structure suited to code, tables and interface text.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Magi Mono applies the restraint and clarity of the Magi family to a fixed-width format, sharpening its compact proportions without losing the soft geometry that keeps extended reading comfortable. The monolinear structure gives an even, dense text image well suited to notes, navigation and structured material. A broad Latin character set covers Western and Central European scripts along with Baltic, Turkish, Celtic and Sami languages, and three stylistic sets offer alternate forms for the lowercase a, zero, and percent and per mille signs.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Magi Mono as the monospaced companion to Magi, carrying the same clear, open and measured voice into a more regular structure. They note that the fixed-width format sharpens the family's existing compactness, giving a steadier line and denser text image, and that the typeface moves between editorial systems, digital products, navigation and structured text while maintaining an even, measured character throughout.

name: Store Norske Ballong
classification_notes: Constructed entirely from five circles, Ballong is a geometric display typeface with a bubbly, rounded character that places it firmly in the display category rather than any text-oriented tradition.
summary: A bold display typeface by Arve Båtevik, constructed from just five circles, producing a bubbly, rhythmic quality suited to posters and editorial headlines.
description: Built from five strictly arranged circles, this single-weight display face achieves an unlikely dynamism despite its geometric constraints. Small joins between letterforms generate rhythm across headlines, while the rounded forms shift in character depending on context, reading variously as smoke, jelly or water. Language support covers selected Western European and Nordic languages, with OpenType alternates including variant S, Z, 2 and 5 forms via a single stylistic set.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Ballong as a bold display font that floats off the page, inspired by balloons and constructed from just five simple, strictly arranged circles. Despite those constraints, they say it bursts with life, movement and personality, with small connections between letters creating a lively rhythm. They liken it to a chameleon, shifting from puffs of smoke to wobbly jelly to flowing water, and note its popularity for posters, album covers and editorial projects.

name: Store Norske Jazz
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a contrasted sans serif, blending rational modernist grotesque structure with warmer, more humanist rhythms. The visible stroke contrast sets it apart from neutral grotesques or pure humanist sans families.
summary: A contrasted sans by Arve Båtevik that holds rational modernist structure in tension with a warmer, more approachable tone suited to identity, editorial and interface work.
description: Drawn from a dialogue between modernist grotesques and warmer lettering traditions, Jazz maintains crisp lines and measured proportions without retreating into anonymity. Its contrast and softer rhythm make it credible across demanding fields such as medicine, finance and technology as well as cultural and editorial contexts where tone matters as much as clarity. A monospaced companion, Jazz Mono, extends the family into typewriter-inspired territory.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Jazz as a contrasted sans serif developed from rational modernist grotesques and warmer lettering traditions, intended for situations where trust and clarity are required but the voice must not become cold. They position it for identities, editorial work, interfaces and cultural communication, as well as more demanding sectors such as medicine, finance and technology.

name: Store Norske Tango
classification_notes: Tango is a monolinear geometric sans with strictly equal horizontal and vertical stroke weights, inspired by Intertype Vogue (1930) and 1920s European geometric type trends, giving it a raw, primitive quality distinct from humanist or grotesque traditions.
summary: A geometric sans by Arve Båtevik drawing on Intertype Vogue (1930) and the American strand of 1920s European geometric type, built from raw, monolinear forms with minimal optical correction.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik for Store Norske Skriftkompani, Tango takes Intertype Vogue as its starting point and departs substantially, prioritising primitive geometric shapes and a monolinear structure where horizontal and vertical strokes hold equal weight, producing an optical suggestion of reverse contrast. Two italic styles are offered: a conventional slant at 10° and a second at 20° that nods to early sans serif conventions, giving designers an expressive range within the family. Its unguarded, naïve quality suits headline work, bold branding and contemporary visual identities across a broad Latin character set covering Western, Central and Northern European scripts including Sami languages.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Tango as a geometric typeface channelling clarity, playfulness and a modern sensibility, inspired by Intertype Vogue (1930), itself an American response to the geometric type trends of 1920s Europe. They credit its designers with pursuing a fresh, unconstrained approach free from European tradition, yielding a typeface of naïve energy, primitive geometric forms and visual purity suited to branding and digital projects.

name: Store Norske Jazz Mono
classification_notes: A fixed-width typeface with contrasted stroke structure and warm, restrained drawing, placing it firmly in the monospaced category while retaining the serif character of its companion Jazz.
summary: A monospaced companion to Store Norske Jazz, retaining the original's contrasted structure and warm restraint within a fixed-width, typewriter-inspired form.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Jazz Mono carries the proportions and controlled calm of Jazz into a fixed-pitch rhythm suited to editorial systems, interfaces and technical fields such as medicine, finance and technology. Each character occupies the same width while preserving enough warmth to keep the typeface from feeling anonymous or coldly mechanical. Three stylistic sets offer alternates for the zero, lowercase a, i/l forms and percent symbols, alongside a full suite of OpenType features for professional typesetting.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Jazz Mono as the monospaced companion to Store Norske Jazz, engineered for clarity and consistency in technical and editorial contexts. They note that the fixed-width form brings interval, alignment and repetition into view while retaining the warmth and controlled tone of Jazz, making it suitable for demanding fields where information must remain steady, legible and credible.

name: Store Norske Geita
classification_notes: The typeface is explicitly described as a geometric grotesk, rooted in a reinterpretation of the display cuts of Akzidenz Grotesk, combining geometric construction with expressive stroke endings.
summary: A geometric grotesk drawn from the display cuts of Akzidenz Grotesk, distinguished by trumpet-shaped stroke endings and offered in six weights with matching italics and a monospaced companion.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Geita takes the display cuts of the original Akzidenz Grotesk as its starting point, softening the genre's typical rigidity with expressive trumpet-shaped stroke endings that give the typeface a warmer, more approachable character. Six weights run from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, covering use cases from impactful headlines down to body text. A monospaced companion, Geita Mono, extends the family into typewriter-inspired territory while retaining the same expressive voice.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Geita as a geometric grotesk embodying warmth and approachability, born from a reinterpretation of the display cuts of the original Akzidenz Grotesk. They highlight its trumpet-shaped stroke endings, clean rhythmic forms, and six-weight family with matching italics as making it equally suited to branding, editorial and digital applications. A monospaced companion, Geita Mono, is offered for those needing a typewriter-inspired format in the same expressive character.

name: Store Norske Geita Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced sans-serif with geometric clarity and distinctive trumpet-shaped stroke endings, drawing directly from its proportional counterpart Geita; the typewriter-inspired construction places it firmly in the geometric monospaced category.
summary: A monospaced companion to Geita by Arve Båtevik, carrying the original's geometric structure and trumpet-shaped stroke endings into a typewriter-inspired format across six weights with italics.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Geita Mono translates the proportional Geita's geometric clarity and distinctive stroke endings into a fixed-width format suited to coding, editorial and branding contexts. Six weights with matching italics give the family useful range, from functional code display to more expressive typographic applications. Broad Latin coverage spans Western, Central and Northern European scripts including Nordic and Sami languages, backed by a thorough set of OpenType features including stylistic alternates for the lowercase a, zero and percentage forms.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Geita Mono as a monospaced sans-serif that translates the warmth and distinctive character of Geita into a typewriter-inspired format, combining geometric clarity with expressive trumpet-shaped stroke endings. They position it as equally suited to technical and creative contexts, with carefully calibrated spacing ensuring performance in coding, editorial and branding use, and note its six-weight family with matching italics as providing versatility from code snippets to display applications.

name: Store Norske Graut
classification_notes: Graut originates from a 1930s monospaced lettering alphabet and retains that mono character as its default form, with proportional spacing available as a variant; its geometric, even letterforms place it firmly in the geometric mono tradition.
summary: A 1930s lettering alphabet revived by Arve Båtevik, offering variable spacing that shifts smoothly between monospaced and fully proportional letterforms.
description: Rooted in an alphabet attributed to the obscure Bruno Frost and documented in a 1930s Norwegian lettering handbook, Graut is available in three static cuts — Mono, Middle and Normal — and a variable font that spans the full range between them. The letterforms remain consistent across all spacing modes, giving designers unusual flexibility without sacrificing visual coherence. It has found a following in branding and cultural work where its period character and spacing quirk give it a distinct identity.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Graut as inspired by a monospaced alphabet from a 1930s lettering handbook, attributed to the little-known Bruno Frost. Designed by Arve Båtevik, it is available in three static fonts — Mono, Middle and Normal — plus a variable font covering the full spacing range, and supports an extensive Latin character set spanning Western, Central and Northern European languages including Nordic and Sami scripts.

name: Store Norske Stempel
classification_notes: Derived from the bold, wide variant of Norway's 1929 official number plate lettering standard (Vertikal Jernskrift), the typeface carries a distinctly utilitarian and industrial character suited to display and poster use rather than continuous text.
summary: A revival of the bold number plate lettering used on Norwegian industrial vehicles from 1929, expanded into a full Latin display typeface by Arve Båtevik.
description: Based on the wider, bolder variant of Vertikal Jernskrift, the standard typeface mandated for Norwegian car number plates from 1929, this face was originally produced by two Oslo stamping workshops and used only the letters needed for vehicle classification terms. Båtevik has extended it into a complete alphabet with broad multilingual Latin support, covering languages from Afrikaans to Welsh and including Sami scripts. Developed as part of the Skrift i Oslo project, which works to revive Oslo's visual dialect through traditional sign painting, it suits branding, posters and editorial work that calls for something with genuine historical weight.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Stempel is derived from the lettering on Norway's first official car number plates, regulated from 17 January 1929 under the Vertikal Jernskrift standard. The typeface is based specifically on the bolder, wider variant used on industrial vehicles to display their function. Where the original contained only a handful of letters, Stempel now offers a full alphabet with punctuation and multilingual Latin support. It was developed as part of the Skrift i Oslo project and is designed by Arve Båtevik.

name: Store Norske Jernskrift
classification_notes: Derived from Norway's 1929 Vertikal Jernskrift number plate alphabet, itself influenced by hand-painted road signs; the letterforms carry a raw, stencil-like industrial character suited to display use rather than running text.
summary: A typeface derived from Norway's 1929 official car number plate alphabet, Vertikal Jernskrift, expanded into a full display family for branding and editorial use.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, it grows from the numerals and limited letterforms of Vertikal Jernskrift, the standardised iron-letter alphabet introduced for Norwegian car plates on 17 January 1929. The original design drew on hand-painted road signs of the era, and a bolder, wider variant inspired by the style required on industrial vehicles is also offered. Developed as part of the Skrift i Oslo project to revive Oslo's visual dialect, the typeface now appears in public signage including the Arbeiderpartiet sign at Youngstorget, with extensive Latin and Sami language support and a broad OpenType feature set.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Jernskrift as a typeface derived from the numerals on Norway's first official car number plates, standardised in 1929 under the name Vertikal Jernskrift. While the original included only numerals and limited letters, Jernskrift provides a complete alphabet with punctuation, multilingual support across Western, Central and Northern European languages including Sami, and OpenType features covering alternate forms, fractions, ligatures and more. The typeface emerged from the Skrift i Oslo project and is used for branding, posters and editorial work.

name: Store Norske Skandia Narrow
classification_notes: Rooted in a rediscovered Scandinavian neo-grotesque originally designed for the Norwegian National Railway, Skandia Narrow retains the geometric structure and measured contrast of its wider counterpart in a condensed width.
summary: A narrow companion to Skandia, itself rooted in a rediscovered Norwegian railway grotesque, designed to hold the character of the original in tighter settings.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Skandia Narrow compresses the proportions of Skandia while preserving its balanced contrast, measured tone and geometric structure. The result is a denser text image with a firmer vertical rhythm, suited to editorial systems, signage and interfaces where space is constrained but the typeface still needs to feel open and stable. It forms part of a broader family that includes the standard Skandia, a condensed cut and a mono variant.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Skandia Narrow as carrying the same balanced contrast, measured tone and geometric structure as Skandia in a tighter width, developed as part of the family rather than as a separate interpretation. The aim was to keep the character of the original forms as present as possible as the proportions narrow, making it well suited to editorial systems, signage and interfaces where space is limited but the typeface still needs to feel open, stable and recognisably Skandia.

name: Store Norske Skandia Condensed
classification_notes: Derived from a Scandinavian neo-grotesque originally designed for the Norwegian National Railway, the condensed cut retains the geometric calm and balanced contrast of the parent family, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque tradition.
summary: A mildly condensed cut of Arve Båtevik's Skandia, rooted in a rediscovered neo-grotesque designed for the Norwegian National Railway.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Skandia Condensed narrows the footprint of the Skandia family without pushing it into compressed territory, preserving the balanced contrast and geometric calm of the original. The cut is positioned for editorial systems, identities and signage where column economy matters but visual continuity with the wider family remains important. Broad Latin coverage extends to Nordic, Sami, Baltic, Celtic and Turkish scripts, with a full OpenType feature set including stylistic alternates for Æ and IJ forms.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske describe Skandia Condensed as a more compact member of the Skandia family for settings that require a narrower fit while staying close to the tone and structure of the original. Only mildly condensed, it preserves Skandia's balanced contrast and geometric calm, making it suitable for identities, editorial systems, signage, and space-conscious applications where a clear relationship to the wider family is needed.

name: Store Norske Litteratur Text
classification_notes: A low-contrast old-style serif drawing on Norwegian and Scandinavian anthology typography from the late 19th and early 20th centuries, with references to typefaces such as Nordisk Antikva and Plantin. The Text variant reduces contrast specifically for dense body text conditions.
summary: A low-contrast text version of Litteratur, drawn by Arve Båtevik and rooted in Scandinavian anthology typography of the late 19th and early 20th centuries.
description: Where the standard Litteratur carries more optical contrast, this Text variant reduces it to suit dense layouts and extended reading. The large x-height and short ascenders and descenders pack more text into tight measures, while special ligatures support Nordic language rhythm. Language coverage spans Western and Central European, Baltic, Nordic and Sami scripts, with a rich set of OpenType features including small caps, multiple figure styles and localised forms.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Litteratur as beginning with an examination of Norwegian literary anthologies from the country's golden age, when authors including Ibsen, Bjørnson, Hamsun and Undset were active. Those anthologies featured sturdy old-style serifs such as Nordisk Antikva and Plantin, and Litteratur aims to revive that warm, inviting atmosphere in a crisp modern form. The Text variant is a heavier, low-contrast version intended specifically for dense layouts and varied reading conditions.

name: Store Norske Baguette
classification_notes: An all-caps headline typeface with variable inclination, designed explicitly for expressive, experimental typographic arrangements rather than body text; its cultural blending concept and dynamic slant axis place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An all-caps variable display typeface by Arve Båtevik, with inclinations of up to 16 degrees in either direction drawn from French, Norwegian and Japanese signage traditions.
description: Designed around the idea that the Latin alphabet is interpreted differently across cultures, Baguette takes cues from French signage, Norwegian typographic conventions and Japanese rereadings of Western letterforms. The family offers one upright style and four inclinations, two leaning left and two right, with the variable font allowing continuous adjustment up to 16 degrees in either direction. Intended for branding and editorial contexts where mixing multiple inclinations in a single layout can produce distinctive, dynamic compositions.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Baguette as a typeface celebrating the diversity of cultural interpretations within the Latin alphabet, drawing on French signage, Norwegian typographic traditions and Japanese reinterpretations of Western letterforms. They frame the concept around the idea that typefaces, like bread, are universal but shaped by cultural context, and highlight the variable font's inclination axis as an invitation for designers to experiment with playful, cross-cultural typographic arrangements.

name: Store Norske Neon
classification_notes: A geometric uppercase-oriented display typeface derived from a mid-century Norwegian sign-maker's metal standard letter system, with structural holes referencing neon tube entry and exit points.
summary: A digital revival of the Metall Standardbokstaver alphabet used by Norwegian neon sign makers, with four styles that can be layered to simulate a physical neon sign.
description: Drawn from the character maps and metal letter kits used by Neon Electric Limited AS, a leading mid-century Norwegian sign supplier whose work included the 1952 Winter Olympics and the Oslo Main Public Library. The typeface comes in four styles: a version with the characteristic tube-entry holes, a solid, an outline and an inline, the latter two designed to be combined so the sign body and the glowing neon tube read as distinct layers. Originally developed for the Skrift i Oslo exhibition, the digital form preserves the rhythmic geometric quality of the holes as a deliberate visual feature rather than a technical artefact.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Neon as a typeface based on the Metall Standardbokstaver alphabet used by sign makers at Neon Electric Limited AS, who carried a suitcase of functional neon pipes and a character map to show clients possible letterforms and colour combinations. The distinctive holes in the letters were practical entry and exit points for neon tubes; in digital form they create a rhythmic effect within the geometric structure. The typeface is available in four styles — holes, solid, outline and inline — which can be layered to simulate a sign body and neon tube, with the tube colour customisable for specific design needs.

name: Store Norske Funksjon
classification_notes: Constructed entirely from circles, squares and triangles with a modular colour-layer system, this is a display typeface in the geometric tradition, designed explicitly for poster and editorial use rather than text setting.
summary: A modular geometric display typeface by Arve Båtevik, reviving a 1933 Oslo shop-front alphabet with a layered two-colour system.
description: Drawn from Knut Schjefstad's 1933 window-decoration guide Moderne Vindusreklame, itself adapted from an earlier Eric Mollowitz design, Funksjon translates that vernacular Oslo lettering tradition into a strict geometry of circles, squares and triangles. Each letter is split into left and right halves available as separate cuts, allowing designers to layer contrasting colours; the Open style offers a single outlined contour, while Natt and Dag each present one open and one closed side per letter. Coverage is compact Western European and Nordic Latin, suited to posters, branding and editorial work that calls for bold, playful geometry.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Funksjon as a geometric display colour typeface celebrating traditional shop-front lettering, rooted in Schjefstad's Moderne Vindusreklame and the Oslo Vocational School for Commerce curriculum. The colour system splits each letter into two independently colourable halves, with Open, Natt and Dag styles offering further compositional variation across one or two colours.

name: Store Norske Bygg
classification_notes: Bygg is a monospaced display typeface derived from hand-painted construction company signage; its fixed-width structure and display origins place it squarely in the display mono subcategory.
summary: A two-weight monospaced display font drawn from photographs of a 1920s–30s Oslo construction company's hand-painted signage.
description: Arve Båtevik derived Bygg from lettering found in archive photographs of Frimann Bye & Winsvold AS, a mortar and construction materials supplier that operated in Oslo during the interwar period. It began as part of the 'Skrift i Oslo' exhibition, which documented the city's sign painting tradition, and eventually grew into a complete functional alphabet. The result is a fixed-width display face covering Western European, Nordic and basic Latin languages, available in two weights.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Bygg as a typeface inspired by lettering found in photographs of Frimann Bye & Winsvold AS's offices, a company that supplied mortar and construction materials in Oslo during the 1920s and 1930s. The face originated in the 'Skrift i Oslo' exhibition to show how modern type design could digitally restore Oslo's visual dialect, and has since evolved into a two-weight monospace font with a compact Latin display character set.

name: Store Norske Samvirke
classification_notes: Drawn from gold-leaf glass signage with a three-dimensional stone-carving effect, the letterforms are decorative display faces rooted in early twentieth-century Norwegian commercial lettering tradition.
summary: A three-style display typeface reviving the gold-leaf window lettering of a 1916 Oslo cooperative grocery store, designed by Arve Båtevik.
description: Based on the gilt glass signage of the Oslo Samvirkelag store in the Kampen district, the design mimics the layered, stone-carved appearance of the original letters. Three styles offer distinct moods: Karva reproduces the shadowing of the source sign, Solid fills the letterforms for maximum impact, and Open traces only the outer contours for a lighter effect. Coverage is compact, targeting display use in branding, posters and editorial headlines with Western European and Nordic language support.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Samvirke as inspired by the gold-leaf lettering of the Oslo Samvirkelag, a cooperative founded in 1916 that became a predecessor to Coop Norge. The typeface originated from the 'Skrift i Oslo' exhibition, which explored Oslo's sign painting tradition, and evolved into a full functional alphabet available in three styles: Karva, Solid and Open, suited to branding, posters and editorial design.

name: Store Norske Stilig
classification_notes: A display typeface with layered, chromatic construction inspired by phototype display lettering; classified as display due to its purely decorative, expressive character and lack of text weights.
summary: A colour font designed by Arve Båtevik, inspired by Andy Song's 1972 phototype face Indigo, offered in a vibrant layered colour style and four distinct monochrome variants.
description: Based on Indigo, a photographic display face created by Andy Song for Studios Hollenstein Phototypo in Paris, Stilig brings that heritage into a contemporary colour font format with a layered, chromatic palette. The four monochrome styles — Dark, Light, Solid and Open — each present the letterforms differently, from bold filled forms with a white rainbow accent to bare outlines, giving designers flexible options for silk screen and riso printing. Character coverage is compact, spanning uppercase, lowercase, selected accented Latin characters and basic punctuation, suited to Western European and Nordic languages.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Stilig is a colour font drawing on the phototype tradition, specifically Andy Song's Indigo (1972), designed for Studios Hollenstein Phototypo in Paris. Its Colour style uses a vibrant palette and layered components for playful typographic combinations, while four monochrome styles — Dark, Light, Solid and Open — each offer distinct visual treatments. The font has found particular use in silk screen and riso printing, as well as posters, logos and record covers.

name: Store Norske Bass
classification_notes: Bass is strictly monospaced with each character occupying uniform width and height, derived from a lettering alphabet in Lettera (1954–72). Its bold, grid-like construction and use in posters and record covers places it firmly in display rather than text territory.
summary: A monospaced display typeface derived from Armin Haab and Walter Hættenschweiler's Lettera alphabet, expanded by Arve Båtevik into a full Latin extended character set.
description: Each glyph occupies an identical footprint, giving Bass a modular, grid-driven rhythm suited to posters and record covers. Arve Båtevik's digital expansion adds diacritics, punctuation and symbols absent from the 1954–72 Lettera source, extending coverage to Western, Central and Northern European scripts including Sami languages. The bold visual presence and monospaced uniformity invite typographic play without sacrificing legibility at display sizes.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Bass builds on a lettering alphabet from Lettera (1954–72) by Armin Haab and Walter Hættenschweiler. Digitally adapted by Arve Båtevik, it expands the original A–Z and 0–9 with new glyphs for a full Latin extended character set, including diacritics, punctuation and symbols. Strictly monospaced, it has proved popular for record covers and posters thanks to its striking, bold visual presence.

name: Store Norske Mekaniske
classification_notes: Derived from workshop lettering at Aker Mekaniske Verksted circa 1930–1931, the typeface carries the functional, stencil-adjacent character of industrial signage rather than any conventional text or grotesque tradition.
summary: A display typeface drawn from early twentieth-century workshop lettering at Norway's largest shipyard, Aker Mekaniske Verksted in Oslo.
description: Arve Båtevik's typeface traces its origins to photographs of lettering at Akers Mek., the Oslo shipyard founded in 1841 that grew to become Norway's largest before closing in 1982. The source material, documented around 1930–1931 by photographer Anders Beer Wilse, gives the design a direct industrial character that sits apart from conventional revivals. Language support spans Western and Northern European scripts including Nordic and Sami languages, and OpenType features include alternates, ligatures and localised forms.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Mekaniske as capturing the enduring industrial spirit of Aker Mekaniske Verksted's original workshop lettering. They trace the shipyard's history from its founding in Fossveien in 1841 through its move to Holmen at Pipervika in 1854, and its eventual closure in 1982, positioning the typeface as a document of Oslo's industrial heritage.

name: Store Norske Trafikk
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a constructed sans-serif, drawing on the lineage of DIN 1451, British Transport and the Norwegian Trafikkalfabetet of 1965, placing it firmly in the engineered, geometric-rational tradition of wayfinding typefaces.
summary: A constructed sans-serif rooted in Norwegian road signage tradition, drawing on Trafikkalfabetet, DIN 1451 and British Transport as its chief references.
description: Designed by Arve Båtevik, Trafikk extends and refines the visual language of Karl Petter Sandbæk's 1965 Trafikkalfabetet, developed for the Norwegian Public Roads Administration. The family is currently available in Medium and Medium Italic, with broad Latin Extended coverage spanning Nordic, Baltic, Celtic, Central and South-Eastern European scripts, as well as Sami languages. Its constructed, rational letterforms are well suited to signage, wayfinding and institutional applications where clear legibility at distance is required.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describe Trafikk as a constructed sans-serif rooted in the visual tradition of Norwegian road signage, inspired by the 1965 Trafikkalfabetet and influenced by both DIN 1451 and British Transport. The typeface draws on core characteristics from these predecessors to develop and refine that heritage, and is now available with full Latin Extended character support in Medium and Medium Italic styles.

name: Arve Båtevik
classification_notes: This page is a designer biography rather than a single typeface; no single category applies, so display is used as a fallback given the foundry's primary output.
summary: Arve Båtevik is the Oslo-based designer and founder of Store Norske Skriftkompani, whose practice pairs archival research into Norwegian visual history with an extensive catalogue of original typefaces.
description: Båtevik holds an MA in Art Direction and Type Design from ECAL in Lausanne, where his diploma project received the Prix David Rust, and a BA in Graphic Design from Westerdals in Oslo. His research runs from a BA thesis featuring interviews with François Rappo, Erik Spiekermann and Christian Schwartz, to 'Den Norske Bokstaven', a grant-funded investigation into Norwegian type design history. His typefaces, several of which have received Gold at the Grafill Visuelt awards, are used by clients including Louis Vuitton, The New York Times Magazine, Adobe and the Munch Museum.
foundry_blurb: Store Norske Skriftkompani describes Arve Båtevik as a designer whose practice is grounded in historical research and cultural preservation, pursuing an inclusive approach to visual culture that bridges diverse visual heritage with contemporary needs. His work balances technical precision, contextual analysis, and openness to experimentation, and his typefaces often emerge as the tangible results of deeper cultural inquiry.
